# Introduction

The ?aayaaqa (Herring) Spawn Dynamics project is a
multi-year collaboration between Uu-a-thluk Fisheries and
Landmark Fisheries Research focused on developing a
Nuu-chah-nulth approach to ecosystem-based fisheries
management (EBFM) for Pacific Herring on the West Coast
of Vancouver Island (WCVI). In the first year of the project
(Phase 1), empirical analyses of data collected during herring
spawn events showed that egg production per gram of body
weight (fecundity) increases with age, and that older herring
spawn earlier in the season [@lfr2024_NTC]. A conceptual
simulation model was also developed to represent WCVI herring
population dynamics across nine Nuu-chah-nulth (NCN) spawning
areas.

In the second year (Phase 2), modelling efforts expanded the
suite of WCVI herring models to include predation mortality,
spatial stock structure, and age-dependent egg production
[@lfr2025_NTC]. Two models were developed: a three-stock
model (*SISCAH-3S-Pred*) fit to data for Pacific Fishery
Management Areas (Stat Areas) 23, 24, and 25, and a nine-stock
simulation model (*SISCAH-9S-Pred*) calibrated from the
three-stock model. Phase 2 concluded that the three-stock
model is the most practical tool for MSE moving forward,
as the nine-stock model was largely insensitive to its
additional spatial and temporal detail, reflecting analyst
assumptions rather than inherent properties of the system.
The nine-stock model remains useful in an empirical
capacity, similar to an exceptional circumstances
framework, for monitoring spawn dynamics at finer spatial
scales than the assessment model considers.

The present working paper describes activities in the third
year (Phase 3) of the project. Building on the Phase 2
recommendation, we use the *SISCAH-3S-Pred* model as an
operating model within a closed-loop simulation framework to
test a potential WCVI herring re-opening procedure. This
represents a key step toward the project's goal of evaluating
whether harvest strategies at varying spatial and temporal
scales can reduce conservation risks and improve harvest
opportunities for WCVI herring.

## Management context

WCVI herring commercial fisheries have been closed since 2006.
A re-opening of the fishery would require a management
procedure (MP) consistent with the DFO Precautionary
Approach framework [@dfo2009pa] that defines how stock
status is assessed and how a total allowable catch (TAC)
is determined [@dfo2024_herringIFMP]. Current BC
herring management procedures are developed and tested using
single-stock operating models that treat WCVI herring as a
panmictic population [@johnson2024SCAH; @DFO_2021a]. However,
the *SISCAH-3S-Pred* model from Phase 2 suggests that the
true population may be spatially structured and subject to
higher natural mortality due to predation than is assumed by
the standard density-dependent mortality (DDM) model used in
DFO assessments.

A robust MP should perform acceptably not only under the
assumptions of the assessment model it is paired with, but
also under alternative hypotheses about population dynamics.
Management strategy evaluation (MSE) is a simulation-based
approach for testing MPs under such uncertainty
[@butterworth1999experiences; @punt2016management]. In an MSE,
an operating model (OM) represents the "true" state of the
fishery system, and the MP is applied in a feedback loop where
data are generated by the OM, the assessment model estimates
stock status from those data, a harvest control rule (HCR)
sets the TAC, and the OM is updated with the resulting catch.
The process is repeated over a projection period, and MP
performance is evaluated against conservation and fishery
objectives.

## Objectives

In this phase, we develop a closed-loop simulation framework
and use it to test a proposed minimum escapement harvest
control rule for WCVI herring under three operating models of
increasing ecological complexity:

1. The *SISCAH-DDM* density-dependent mortality model, which
   represents the assumptions currently used in DFO stock
   assessments;

2. The *SISCAH-1S-Pred* single-stock model with explicit
   predation mortality [@doherty2024predation], which adds
   predation but retains a single-stock structure; and

3. The *SISCAH-3S-Pred* three-stock model with predation
   [@lfr2025_NTC], which adds both predation and spatial
   stock structure.

The *SISCAH-3S-Pred* model serves as a robustness test
operating model. When the MP assumes a single panmictic stock
but the true population is spatially structured, we can
evaluate how well the MP protects individual Stat Area stocks
and how spatial TAC allocation methods affect outcomes.
We test three allocation methods for distributing the
aggregate TAC among Stat Areas: (i) proportional to recent spawn
indices, (ii) proportional to recent catches, and
(iii) allocating the entire TAC to Stat Area 23 (Barkley Sound).
